data.title: Introducing The S&P 500 Realized Dispersion Indices
data.description:
Market participants are continuously seeking innovative tools to better understand market dynamics and manage risk. This week marks the launch of the S&P 500 Realized Dispersion Indices. These indices are designed to measure the historical dispersion of The 500 over various time periods, providing valuable insights into market volatility and the performance of individual stocks relative to the broader market. They also serve as an analytical complement to the Cboe S&P 500 Dispersion Index (DSPX), which measures implied dispersion over the next 30 days.

Understanding Dispersion and Its Importance

Dispersion is an important statistical measure that quantifies the range of outcomes among the components of an index during a specific period. By evaluating how individual stocks perform in relation to the overall index, it’s possible to gain insights into individual patterns and underlying risks.

The S&P 500 Realized Dispersion Indices offer two primary measures: the S&P 500 1-Day Realized Dispersion Index, which reflects historical dispersion over a single trading day; and the S&P 500 30-Day Realized Dispersion Index, which provides insights over a rolling 30-calendar day period. The full universe is used for the S&P 500 1-Day Realized Dispersion Index, while the 30-day version is limited to stocks that remain in the index for the entire rolling period and maintain non-zero weights; corporate actions like spin-offs are excluded to avoid distortions. These indices utilize daily and monthly returns, as well as adjusted close weights of each S&P 500 constituent, alongside the daily and monthly returns of The 500 itself. This comprehensive approach makes it possible for market participants to gain a nuanced understanding of market behavior and the performance of individual stocks.

Exhibits 1a & 1b show the historical hypothetical levels of the index over the period for which data is available. The spikes highlight volatile market conditions where individual stocks experienced varying degrees of price movements, which often resulted in greater differences in performance. This divergence in stock returns relative to one another typically manifested as increased dispersion, making patterns and underlying risks across the market easier to observe.

It’s useful to highlight the difference between macro volatility and dispersion, particularly through the lens of historical market events. Dispersion reflects how differently individual stocks move from one another, while volatility captures the overall intensity of market movement. Correlation measures the degree to which two securities move in relation to each other. During the Black Monday crash of 1987 and the Dot-Com burst of March 2000, dispersion reached an all-time high as stock performance diverged significantly—some soared while some crashed, driven by lower correlations. In contrast, events like the Global Financial Crisis of 2008 and COVID in 2020 were marked by high volatility and high correlations, where most stocks moved in the same direction. Recognizing these patterns could provide insights into the fundamental drivers of equity market risk and return.

Exhibit 2 compares the S&P 500 30-Day Realized Dispersion Index with the DSPX, highlighting the relationship between historical and implied dispersion over time. Realized dispersion reflects the variability of individual stock returns over the past 30 days, while DSPX reflects the market’s expectation for dispersion over the next 30 days. The two series generally moved together but there were notable gaps during periods of heightened uncertainty. These divergences can offer insights into shifts in market sentiment and can help identify when the market overestimated or underestimated future stock return differences.

Conclusion: A Practical Addition to the Investment Analytics Toolkit

The launch of the S&P 500 Realized Dispersion Indices marks a notable development in the tools available to investors seeking to navigate market volatility. By providing a transparent measure of realized dispersion, these indices may help investors better understand the underlying dynamics of risk and return in the U.S. equity market.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AMD: Catalysts Align Ahead Of A 2025 Inflection Point
data.description:
The divergence observed between Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) stock's underperformance and its improving underlying business fundamentals this year has created a compelling opportunity. Despite a broader industry selloff driven by China-related regulatory uncertainties and tariff risks, AMD delivered a standout Q1 and robust FY25 outlook that reinforces the strength of its core business.

This widening disconnect has pushed AMD’s valuation toward levels we view as deeply discounted relative to its comparable peers. With sector-specific policy clarity likely to emerge in the second half of the year, and multiple company-specific catalysts on the horizon for AMD, the setup for a fundamental-driven rebound is increasingly clear. We believe AMD is primed for a V-shaped recovery, with current levels offering an attractive long-term entry point ahead of what could be a pivotal 12 months.

Narrowing the Competitive Gap

AMD’s data center business is emerging as a cornerstone of its long-term growth story, with the momentum behind its next-generation products helping to narrow the competitive gap in the AI compute race. While market attention has centered on the ramp-up of AMD’s Instinct AI GPU sales, strong adoption of its Zen 5 EPYC “Turin” CPUs has also surprisingly remained a bright spot. Specifically, EPYC demand remains resilient across both hyperscaler and enterprise deployments, driven by the chips’ compelling total cost of ownership (“TCO”) for general-purpose workloads and their versatility in retrofitting legacy infrastructure for AI applications.

This robust traction for AMD’s EPYC server CPUs lays a solid foundation for its next leg of data center expansion, anchored by the upcoming launch of higher-performance Instinct MI350 series GPUs in 2H25. Specifically, the MI350 series GPUs are expected to serve as a vital growth offset amid rising uncertainties in China tied to U.S. export restrictions.

Recall that the company has recently received word from the government on the restricted sale of its Instinct MI308 GPU – products previously tailored specifically for the Chinese market. The policy shift has triggered an $800 million inventory-related write-down, dealing a direct hit to AMD’s 2Q profit outlook. More significantly, the adverse development effectively eliminates AMD’s access to an estimated $1.5 billion in near-term revenue opportunities from China, underscoring the growing financial impact of escalating export controls, which remains a wildcard for the industry.

This underscores the critical importance of AMD’s upcoming upgrade cycle, led by the next-generation Instinct MI350 series GPUs. Early interest has been strong, driven by the performance and efficiency gains enabled by AMD’s latest CDNA 4 architecture – reinforcing confidence in the product’s potential to accelerate data center growth. Looking further ahead, the MI400 series – set to debut in 2026 – is expected to mark a more transformative step. Designed to better compete with Nvidia’s (NVDA) Blackwell GPUs and bundled with rack-scale server solutions via AMD’s newly acquired ZT Systems unit, the MI400 launch will deepen AMD’s penetration into the $500 billion AI accelerator market. This evolution not only unlocks incremental revenue but also enhances AMD’s data center margin profile and cash generation potential, further strengthening its valuation outlook.

Tariff Clarity Pivot

Lingering uncertainty over semiconductor sector tariffs has become a persistent overhang on valuations observed across the broader semiconductor sector. While the upcoming sector-specific tariffs are expected to be less severe than the sweeping, country-specific levies announced by President Trump in early April, the lack of visibility into final figures continues to weigh on investor sentiment.

However, much of the anticipated downside appears already priced into AMD’s current market performance. The stock fell over 30% following the Trump administration’s formal tariff announcement via the February 1 White House fact sheet, before partially rebounding in early April amid the temporary pause on reciprocal tariffs and indications of a more measured approach to semiconductor duties. Our downside scenario analysis, detailed in later sections, also supports the view that AMD’s valuation has found a defensible floor at the low $100-range.

With tariff decisions expected to crystallize within the coming months, we believe the narrative will shift from tariff “impact” to tariff “clarity.” This transition is poised to lift a key risk overhang on sector multiples and redirect investor attention toward AMD’s product-driven fundamental upside. The timing aligns well with the company’s upcoming ramp-up of next-generation Instinct MI350 and MI400 series AI GPUs, and broader data center momentum. This accordingly positions the impending sectoral tariff resolution for semiconductors as a potential accelerant to AMD’s recovery.

Strategic Upside from ZT Systems

AMD’s timely acquisition of ZT Systems, finalized in late March, has proven to be a prescient strategic move – positioning the company to deepen its reach into the growing AI infrastructure market, while also unlocking unexpected financial upside. While the acquisition included ZT’s vertically integrated U.S.-based manufacturing arm, AMD made clear from the start that its primary interest lay in integrating ZT’s server solution expertise – and not in operating its manufacturing footprint. The plan was always to divest the manufacturing component post-close.

What initially appeared to be a routine two-step transaction has now turned into a high-upside opportunity. ZT Systems’ facilities, which have historically supplied systems to hyperscalers like Microsoft, are now drawing strong interest from foreign server vendors – particularly those based in Taiwan – seeking a U.S. manufacturing base to mitigate the rising cost and demand risks posed by reciprocal and sector-specific tariffs.

AMD management recently noted “significant interest in ZT’s manufacturing business,” suggesting that a sale is likely nearing completion – and at an attractive premium. The expected divestiture not only supports AMD’s asset-light strategy, but also stands to deliver stronger-than-anticipated cash flow accretion. When paired with the core strategic goal of bundling ZT’s rack-scale server capabilities with AMD’s CPUs and GPUs, the transaction strengthens both AMD’s competitive positioning and its fundamental-driven valuation outlook. This adds yet another layer to the company’s multi-pronged tailwind ahead.

Fundamental Considerations

Updating our previous forecast for AMD’s actual 1Q25 performance and forward outlook, we expect its full year 2025 revenue to expand 30% y/y to $33.4 billion. Specifically, data center segment sales are expected to remain AMD’s key growth driver ahead, despite near-term China headwinds related to the sales ban on Instinct MI308 accelerators through 3Q. We expect data center segment sales to reaccelerate in the second half of the year, driven by the ramp-up of next-generation MI350 series GPUs alongside robust EPYC Turin uptake across enterprise and hyperscaler verticals. And despite the heightened growth base anticipated in 2H25, data center segment sales are expected to remain in strong double-digit percentage growth through FY26, as the deployment of more competitive MI400 series GPUs curated for increasing inferencing workloads start to scale.

As a result, we expect AMD’s gross margins to rebound from the $800 million cost headwind related to the MI308 export curb, and re-expand toward recent highs by year-end. Additional upside is likely, driven by a favorable mix shift toward higher-margin data center sales and growing demand for premium, higher-ASP offerings across AMD’s client, gaming, and embedded segments. These dynamics are set to amplify operating leverage gains as the company scales deployment of its next-generation product roadmap, reinforcing the strength of its margin and earnings recovery trajectory.

Valuation Considerations

We are maintaining our base case price target for AMD at $200 per share, which would represent 2x upside from current levels.

The price target is computed using the discounted cash flow (“DCF”) method, incorporating projections outlined in the preceding fundamental analysis. A WACC of 9.6% is applied, which is in line with AMD’s capital structure and risk profile. The analysis also considers a terminal value based on a 3.5% perpetual growth rate applied to projected 2034 EBITDA, which proxies terminal cash flows at steady-state. This growth assumption aligns with the upper range of long-term economic expansion prospects across AMD’s core operating regions, and reflects the company’s enduring role as a key driver of innovation and productivity in the global tech ecosystem, even at steady-state.

Final Thoughts

In our view, AMD offers a compelling long-term investment opportunity at current levels. Trading around $102 post-earnings, the stock remains below even our downside scenario price target of $105, highlighting a favorable risk-reward setup. This conservative downside case assumes a 25% haircut to AMD’s baseline FY25 growth projections, reflecting further adverse implications from looming tariff and regulatory uncertainties, while holding key valuation assumptions constant (9.6% WACC and 3.5% terminal growth). The adjustment mirrors the downward revision in economists’ 2025 GDP forecasts, from 2.2% earlier in the year to roughly 1.7% today, amid tighter financial conditions.

Yet, as outlined in our analysis, AMD’s fundamentals remain resilient and increasingly supported by a strong pipeline of catalysts – including the upcoming Instinct MI350 and MI400 GPU ramp, anticipated tariff clarity, and strategic value unlocked from the ZT Systems transaction. Together, these drivers set the stage for a sustained V-shaped recovery, underpinned by solid margin expansion, cash flow accretion, and valuation re-rating potential.

data.title: Preferred Stocks To Sell (Part 4): Mid-America Apartment Communities' MAA.PR.I
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

This is our next in-line article, which is dedicated to a currently overvalued, on a relative basis, listed preferred stock. The main purpose of this series of short studies is to show preferred stocks with unattractive yields for the credit quality they offer and little to no upside potential. This time, we will turn our attention to the property REIT Mid-America Apartment Communities, Inc.'s (NYSE:MAA) issued 8.50% Series I Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Shares (NYSE:MAA.PR.I). As usual in this type of analysis, we'll go through the issuer's financials, and after that, we'll put the fixed-income security in a relative value analysis, pointing out the reasons why we believe its current pricing makes it overvalued.

Mid-America Apartment Communities Overview

MAA Communities is a publicly traded real estate investment trust headquartered in Memphis, Tennessee. MAA is focused on the investment performance for its shareholders through the acquisition, development, and management of apartment communities. As of the end of March 2025, MAA holds a portfolio of over 104 thousand apartment homes across 16 states and the District of Columbia.

Founded in 1977, MAA Communities is currently a member of the Fortune 500 list of the biggest U.S. companies.

The price chart below illustrates how the general market has been evaluating the company for the past ten years:

If we compare the total return performance for the same period of MAA Communities to that of the S&P 500 (SP500) and the iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ), as an ETF with a significant exposure to the REIT Residential Sector, we get the following chart:

For the period we observe MAA is slightly outperforming the SP500 index. However, over the shorter time frame, we see that the market managed to make a new high in its total return performance after the end of the 2021 local peak, while MAA is still struggling to reach its 2021 values. In the other comparison, MAA Communities is outperforming the REZ ETF by a lot.

Regarding the future valuation of MAA Communities, both Seeking Alpha analysts and the Quant Rating are giving the company a neutral "Hold" score, while the Wall Street analysts are bullish and grant it the "Buy" rating:

If we take a look at the MAA Communities Factor grades and compare their current values to what they were six months ago, we can get a picture of the company's deterioration in Valuation, Growth, and Profitability metrics. The Growth and Profitability are still in the green, but the Valuation has entered the red zone. The Momentum and Earnings revisions of MAA have improved since half a year ago, with the Momentum valuation having one of the best scores.

Comparing MAA's total return performance against other established names in the residential REIT Sector, we see that over the past ten years, the company was the obvious outlier with virtually no period of time in which it was outperformed by any peer. The chart below shows the most probable reason why MAA is constantly outperforming the REZ ETF.

Company Credibility

Fitch Ratings - New York - 23 Jul 2024: Fitch Ratings has affirmed Mid-America Apartment Communities' ((MAA)) Long-Term Issuer Default Rating ((IDR)) at 'A-' and its preferred stock at 'BBB'. Fitch has affirmed MAA subsidiary Mid-America Apartments L.P.'s Long-Term IDR and its unsecured debt at 'A-'. Fitch has also affirmed the subsidiary's Short-Term IDR and CP rating at 'F1'. The Rating Outlook is Stable.

The rating reflects MAA's strong balance sheet, solid execution of its investment strategy and established capital access. Leverage is currently below Fitch's sensitivities, and we expect it to remain within or below the company's stated net leverage policy of 4.5x-5.0x longer term. This would be consistent with the company's policies and the current rating level.

This is the beginning of the latest rating action commentary, in which Fitch affirms the 'A-' long-term issuer default rating with a stable outlook for MAA Communities. The company's rating history for the past couple of years is depicted below. The last change in the credit quality of MAA that Fitch Ratings made was in May 2022, when the rating was upgraded to the 'A-' from the previous 'BBB+' score. Since then, according to the rating agency, there have been no fundamental changes in the long-term IDR of MAA.

Capital Structure

Mid-America Apartment Communities recently issued an earnings report for the First Quarter of 2025, which shows the company's actual capital structure. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is also provided for comparison purposes, so anyone interested can see how it evolved historically in the short term.

As of Q1'25, MAA Communities had a total debt of $5.1 billion, ranking senior to the outstanding preferred stock equity of approximately $43.4 million. At the lowest position in the company's capital structure is the $5.9 billion of common equity. The calculated this way Debt-to-Equity for MAA Communities amounts to 0.86 - a higher value than the average of 1.4 for the Residential REIT sector in the U.S. in May 2025.

Dividends

MAA Communities has a 14-year history of dividend growth and 26 years of uninterrupted dividend payments. This is what the dividend history of the company looks like for the past ten years:

The twelve-month trailing dividend distributed to MAA's shareholders amounts to $5.97 in the form of quarterly payments. With the current price of the stock at $161.66, that corresponds to a 3.69% dividend yield. This is how the dividend yield looks for the past 10 years:

And the forward estimations for the dividend and dividend yield:

MAA Communities has approximately 117 million common shares outstanding and a $6.06 forward estimated annual payment per share. With this data, we calculate around $709 million annualized forward distribution for the common stockholders. The total $3.8 million annual dividend for the preferred shares of the company represents a tiny fraction of that sum, approximately 0.5%. The cushion that the common shares distribution represents is huge and should make the preferred shareholders feel rather confident that their dividend is well covered.

MAA Communities Bond Issues

The senior debt of MAA Communities is credit-rated "A3" by Moody's, and the different issues, trading on the bond market, with their respective maturity dates are shown below.

The long-term debt of the company, represented by the '51 issue, is priced at approximately 100 basis points above the 30-year treasuries (US30Y), while the November '25 issue is at 60 basis points above the 6-month treasuries (US6M).

The average single-A-rated US corporate debt currently trades at approximately 90 basis points above the corresponding benchmark, so we can state MAA's debt does not deviate from the average values. At the moment, it does not seem that the creditors are seeing some kind of specific problem in MAA Communities that will make them demand a higher risk premium than the other 'A'-rated peers.

MAA-I analysis

Next, we take a closer look at the MAA-I issue itself, its current pricing, and what is our future expectations for it. MAA-I is a fixed distribution rate perpetual traditional preferred stock, credit-rated 'BBB' by S&P and Fitch Ratings. It is a legacy issue from Post Properties Inc., which was rebranded with its current exchange ticker after the merger with MAA in 2016. The nominal yield of the security is 8.5%, it is callable on and after 10/01/2026, and has no stated maturity date.

Currently, MAA-I is trading for $55.29, with a current yield of 7.69% and yield to call of 1.49%.

We need to know how to evaluate MAA-I: as a perpetuity with a 'BBB' credit score and 7.7% CY, which makes it a rather decent investment opportunity, or as a high probability redemption candidate in a year and a half, which makes it a "Strong Sell" candidate. Luckily for us, MAA Communities includes in its regular quarterly report the MAA-I call option evaluation, too, among other things. This is a very important piece of information, because having it, we don't have to guess how the company will react when they have to decide "redemption, or not." We'll just use the same numbers they have calculated to determine whether MAA-I is a high-probability redemption candidate.

The Company has recognized a derivative asset related to the redemption feature embedded in the MAA Series I preferred stock. The derivative asset is valued using widely accepted valuation techniques, including a discounted cash flow analysis in which the perpetual value of the preferred shares is compared to the value of the preferred shares assuming the call option is exercised, with the value of the bifurcated call option as the difference between the two values. The analysis reflects the contractual terms of the redeemable preferred shares, which are redeemable at the Company’s option beginning on October 1, 2026 at the redemption price of $50.00 per share.

....

The redemption feature embedded in the MAA Series I preferred stock is reported as a derivative asset in “Other assets” in the accompanying Condensed Consolidated Balance Sheets and is adjusted to its fair value at each reporting date, with a corresponding non-cash adjustment to “Other non-operating income” in the accompanying Condensed Consolidated Statements of Operations. As of March 31, 2025 and December 31, 2024, the fair value of the embedded derivative was $12.7 million and $13.2 million, respectively.

Source: MAA Q1'25 Report.

Having the fair value of the embedded call option of $12.7 million for the whole issue MAA-I and the count of 867,846 preferred shares issued and outstanding, we can now calculate a $14.63 call option value per preferred share. This is a huge number for a preferred with a $50 PAR value. Anyone holding a security with an embedded call option is "Short," and on the other hand, the issuer is "Long" on that option. I'm in no position to speak for the management team of MAA Communities, but put in their shoes, I cannot think of any sound reason not to exercise the option that is close to 30% of the PAR value "in the money" and refinance the preferred at a lower cost for the company. Let us check how realistic a cheaper refinancing is.

Above are shown the details of one of the latest senior notes issued by MAA Communities. IPO-ed on December 18, 2024, with 10 years until maturity, the security had a risk premium of only 45 basis points above the 10-year treasuries at that time.

Accounting for the lower position in the capital structure that a preferred has, compared to senior debt, and the longer duration of a perpetuity, compared to a 10-year term security, I think 150 basis points above the 30-year treasuries is a realistic risk premium at which MAA Communities could refinance a perpetual preferred stock. With a 30-year treasury yield at 4.84% at the moment, a preferred IPO perpetuity at 6.3%-6.5% is a plausible scenario. I'm aware that there is a year and a half until the possible redemption of MAA-I, and for that time, a lot of things could happen. But at least at the moment, the 8.5% nominal yield that MAA-I has is way too much for a 'BBB'- rated security.

Conclusion

MAA-I is way overvalued, priced at 1.5% YTC. MAA Communities is evaluating the call option of the issue to close to 30% of the PAR value, making the security a rather probable candidate for an early redemption in a year and a half. Even if not redeemed by the company at first opportunity, I'd expect it to trade "pinned to PAR," so the yield-to-call is still the right metric to evaluate it, in my opinion. The MAA's latest debt issues and the yields at which its senior debt is currently priced indicate that the company should have no problem refinancing MAA-I at a considerably lower cost.

data.title: Alibaba Q4 Preview: I'm Not Adding Before The Report
data.description:
Introduction

Alibaba Group (NYSE:BABA) is due to report its Q4 ’25 results on Thursday, May 15th, before the market opens. So, I wanted to take a look at what to expect and what I will be looking for in the report. I am not adding to my position right now, mainly because there is a bit of uncertainty surrounding the macroeconomic outlook, and how the company is going to navigate all of this uncertainty, therefore, I am assigning it a hold rating, which may change once I am comfortable about the company’s future a bit more.

What to Expect

Analysts expect the company to make around $1.77 and $1.13 in non-GAAP and GAAP, respectively, on $33.17B in revenues. This translates to around 8% y/y growth, and -13.5% sequentially. A very typical drop-off for the company. Many enterprises renew their contracts at the end of the year, so December quarters are always much more elevated than the following ones. We can see that y/y it is still growing. Unfortunately, the management doesn’t provide any concrete numbers in terms of outlook, so it is impossible to see how the analysts’ numbers stack up against the company's management. So, it is more likely that we see the numbers either miss or beat estimates. Over the last ten quarters, the company missed analysts’ estimates six times on revenues and twice on EPS. It seems that analysts are usually overestimating the company’s revenue performance.

In my opinion, the company will beat the estimates for the upcoming quarter, as US-China trade tensions and macroeconomic headwinds may be overstated.

It is also worth noting that there have been 9 upward revisions against 3 downward revisions on EPS in the last 90 days. Analysts seem to be betting on a better performance by the company.

What I’m Looking For

In terms of operating performance by the company, I would like to see how its bottom line and EPS performed in the last quarter. It is expected to rise sharply, almost 70% y/y, on the back of improved cost structure and a focus on higher-margin business ventures like cloud, AI, and core e-commerce, as well as exiting some of the less profitable areas of the company, like the offline retail and traditional brick-and-mortar businesses. These required a lot of upfront investments only to deliver subpar returns overall, so turning the focus to e-commerce is a good strategic shift. The company already divested its Sun Art Retail group at the end of 2024, with its next venture going on the chopping block, Intime Retail, which the company announced the sale of back in December 2024. I would like to hear how this is progressing, as we haven’t heard anything in the previous earnings call regarding the sale, whether it is close to being finalized or not. The management said we should hear more in the March or June quarters. Will the company announce further non-core divestments in the segments like Freshippo and RT-Mart sooner rather than later? I would like to say yes, as that would be going along with the company’s strategic shift of focusing on higher-margin businesses.

With this shift in strategy, I am expecting to see a decent improvement in the company’s gross margins going forward. 37% is around the number we should expect to see for the upcoming quarter, and anything above that would be welcome. In the past, BABA’s gross margins were in the mid-40s, so the management has a long way to go to return to such efficiency. It is possible, in my opinion.

Last quarter showed a notable decline in free cash flow due to heavy investments in AI and Cloud infrastructure. I will be paying close attention to the number this time around, too. I don’t think the negative FCF number last quarter was a negative because I applaud companies that are trying to stay not only relevant but also keep their position at the top of the leaderboard, and BABA, at least in China, is still the king of Cloud infrastructure. So, further investments here are positive, as long as the returns are worth the capital investments.

Speaking of Cloud and AI, can the business segment continue to grow at around mid-teens going forward? Last quarter, the cloud segment saw around 13% growth y/y. Since the upcoming quarter is naturally going to be lower than the previous one because of cyclicality, anything over 10% would be fantastic, but I don’t expect that to happen. Unless all the capital investments it made recently here are going to bear early fruit. I’m sure the management will give us an update on the adoption of the Qwen3 AI model, as well as any new partnership it may have in store for the rest of the year.

The company is the top cloud provider in China, and it is not even close, however, it has been stuck at around the 36% to 37% mark for a while now, so I hope that all that extra capex is going to help it push over this plateau in the future.

On to E-commerce, domestically, Taobao and Tmall Group have been performing quite well in the past, and I expect this trend to continue. In terms of top-line performance, 5% expected growth isn’t anything to write home about; however, if we see further margin expansion, then I can forgive the lackluster sales growth, in my opinion, especially if the company’s international e-commerce ventures are going to make up for it. AliExpress, Lazada, and Trendyol have been growing quite well recently, however, there may be some friction because of the US-China relations, especially the tariff war. I would like the management to address this issue that has been the main headline for the last few months now, and whether the company is going to navigate this situation with as little impact as possible. I’m sure the company will manage to find other countries that will buy their products, which will fill the gap that the US may leave.

Also, the company’s AIDC is supposed to achieve its first quarter of profitability in the next fiscal year. I want the management to reiterate that.

In terms of outlook and guidance, weak guidance for the next fiscal year is not going to be received well by the investment community; however, I think many already expect some sort of weakness due to the geopolitical tensions, so a lot of the worries may have been already priced in, and if the company comes out with positive comments on the upcoming fiscal year, expect investors to flock in to the company and boost its share price.

Closing Comments

These are a few things that I will keep an eye out for in the upcoming quarter report, which will come out next Thursday. I am long the company from here; however, since it had quite a decent recovery in the last month or so, I am going to assign it a hold rating for now, until I get a bit more information on the above key metrics that I’m going to be focusing on. I still think the company is going to be a winner in the long run, especially with the dominance it has in China, and if it manages to break through internationally even more, that is just going to be a bonus.

data.title: MKS Instruments Q1: Stabilizing, More Appealing, Yet Too Indebted
data.description:
In January, I last offered my views on MKS Instruments, Inc. (NASDAQ:MKSI) whose services are instrumental in technological advancements. Nearly three years after the Atotech deal announcement, leverage was still very elevated after the industry had seen tougher times in 2024, with earnings power starting to provide support to the valuation.

Earnings growth and deleveraging could provide real support to the shares, but high leverage left the business vulnerable to exogenous shocks, like the tariffs rout seen in recent months.

The company has seen a relatively solid performance in the first quarter, in a very uncertain environment, which in combination with a lower share price improves appeal from an earnings point of view, as leverage remains a bit too high too my taste.

Technologies For A Connected World

MKS has a focus on precision solutions, which have applications in multiple end markets. This is certainly the case where precision, complexity and chemistry all come together, often applications which advance technology and drive innovations.

Application groups include datacenters, automotive, IOT, AI, smartphones and medical, in generally all quite strong growth markets. Continued advancements in EUV and atomic layer processing drive growth in applications across these industries.

The company generated EBITDA margins equal to 25% in 2023 on a $3.6 billion revenue base, with innovative capabilities backed up by nearly 2,000 engineers and scientists employed, and R&D investments exceeding 7% of sales. These innovative capabilities are backed up by more than 3,000 patents.

Half the addressable markets are tied to semiconductors, with the remainder split between specialty industrial and to a smaller extent packaging and electronics. Over half of sales are tied to equipment, complemented by a combined 40% revenue exposure to consumables and services.

The business, like many industry peers, got caught up in a bidding race for its peer Coherent in 2021. While MKS fortunately did not "win" this race, it ended up pursuing another expensive deal, as it bought German-based Atotech in a $6.5 billion deal in 2022, creating a real overhang lasting until today. This came after red-hot end markets in 2021 quickly normalized.

This hurt the business as well, even as a $863 million EBITDA number in 2023 (for margins equal to 25% of sales) is very decent, yet it had to support a huge $3.9 billion net debt load. Through the first three quarters of 2024 the company posted sales of $868 million, $887 million and $896 million, respectively, with revenues trending flattish near $3.6 billion (after the company guided for fourth quarter sales at $910 million).

With net debt coming in flattish around $3.9 billion, leverage remained high, although the earnings power improved to a run rate at $8 per share based on the fourth quarter run rate. Some sequential improvements could boost earnings, which could reduce leverage, which meant that I was gradually seeing room for improvements, but was still cautious amidst the debt employed.

Getting More Interested

In February, MKS reported fourth quarter sales of $935 million, comfortably beating the guidance, with revenues up nearly 5% on the year before. This was encouraging, as adjusted earnings were reported at $2.15 per share, as net debt ticked down to just over $3.8 billion.

This was comforting as fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA came in at $237 million, coming in close to the billion mark on an annualized basis, still resulting in leverage around 4 times. The problem is that the sequential growth is seeing a hiccup with first quarter sales seen around $910 million, adjusted earnings seen at $1.40 per share and EBITDA seen at just $217 million.

In May, MKS reported first quarter sales of $936 million, up a million on a sequential basis and much better than guided for, as the company has been rapidly building up a track record of guiding conservatively here. Adjusted earnings came in at $1.71 per share, but this is down quite a bit on a sequential basis as EBITDA was reported at $236 million, with net debt reported flat at $3.8 billion.

First quarter semiconductor sales were up 18% to $413 million, with electronic & packaging revenues up 22% to $253 million, offset by a 13% fall in specialty industrial sales, notably automotive applications.

The company guided for similar results in the second quarter, with sales seen around $925 million, EBITDA around $216 million and adjusted earnings seen around $1.56 per share. Including some conservativeness in the guidance, this resulted in flattish sequential numbers. This implies earnings around $7 per share, EBITDA around $950 million and leverage reported stubbornly high.

Part of the limited deleverage came as the company bought back $45 million worth of stock in the first quarter.

What Now?

Trading at $77 per share, the nearly 68 million shares of the company grant the company just a $5.2 billion equity valuation, and a $9 billion enterprise valuation. In the light of a $6.5 billion deal for Atotech, it is very clear that years after that deal, the transaction is still causing an overhang on the business.

Frankly, the second quarter guidance looks quite comforting in light of the uncertainty around tariffs, as the guidance is based on all known tariffs announced to date, so the flattish guidance is quite comforting. With earnings power now trending around $6-7 per share, the business looks cheap from an earnings multiple point of view, but debt remains high in part as cash flows are hurt by minimal restructuring efforts initiated during the quarter.

The truth is that the Atotech transaction was still ill-advised, as notably its acquired activities have recently seen some underperformance. However, in reality the valuations have been de-risked a bit as shares trade at essentially the lowest levels since 2017. Trading in the $70s, shares are down a great deal from the highs around the $200 mark around the momentum times in 2021.

While earnings power can justify the current valuation, the problem and challenges are that of debt. which remains quite high. I am a bit surprised by the early share repurchases in the quarter, certainly in a highly uncertain environment.

Fortunately, the company finds itself on somewhat of a better footing, but deleveraging goes slow, which limits my enthusiasm on the shares here.

data.title: Peakstone Realty Trust 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Peakstone Realty Trust in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.
